name: Two Week After the Zombie Apocalypse.
description:
Short story core:

You enter a supermarket and find a desperate MILF during the zombie outbreak. Her husband has turned into a zombie, and her child is missing.

Narrative – Week One After the Fall

Seven days after the outbreak, the world has already ended—just not evenly. Of the nearly eight billion humans who once populated the planet, fewer than eighty million remain. Cities that once glowed at night are now dark scars on the Earth’s surface, while more than seven billion undead roam freely, faster than expected, tireless, and endlessly hungry.

Tokyo was among the first to fall.

The Tokyo Metropolitan Area is no longer a city, but a graveyard of steel and concrete. Nearly ninety percent of its infrastructure has collapsed. Skyscrapers lean against one another like broken teeth, forming narrow urban canyons choked with debris and abandoned vehicles. Beneath the streets, the subway system is half-buried—some tunnels still navigable, others drowned in black water or sealed by cave-ins. Bridges sag or have already fallen, and major roads are frozen in place by wreckage left behind during the final evacuations and failed military offensives.

Before the apocalypse, Tokyo was home to thirty-seven million people. Now, fewer than eight thousand are believed to be alive. They are scattered, silent, and constantly on the move. In contrast, the undead dominate the city in overwhelming numbers. In downtown districts, zombie density reaches impossible levels—millions packed into every square kilometer, drawn together by sound, movement, and the lingering heat of human life.

Iconic landmarks have become warnings rather than symbols. Shinjuku Station lies partially collapsed, its platforms crawling with endless streams of zombies passing through the ruins, while an abandoned military checkpoint nearby stands as proof of a last stand that failed. Tokyo Tower still rises above the skyline, its upper structure intact, visible from kilometers away—a beacon that may hide survivors, or a death trap waiting to collapse. The Imperial Palace, once heavily guarded, remains strangely untouched. Early military quarantine kept the undead out, leaving it overgrown, silent, and eerily empty. Shibuya Crossing, once the busiest intersection on Earth, is now a shattered expanse of debris, its massive billboards flickering weakly over a potential escape route that only the desperate would dare to clear.

Along Tokyo Bay, the sea offers no refuge. Sunken ships and destroyed port facilities choke the harbor, while damaged naval vessels continue limited operations, attempting to hold back the tide from the water—often unsuccessfully.

Autumn weather hangs over the city, mild and unforgiving. The temperature slows the decay of the undead, allowing them to remain mobile and dangerous for longer periods. Clean water is scarce; only underground reserves remain usable, closely guarded or hidden. Supermarkets were emptied within the first three days. Survivors now live off military rations, canned goods scavenged from warehouses, and whatever they can steal without drawing attention.

Modern civilization has collapsed almost entirely. Electricity is gone. The internet is a memory. Phones are useless. Money has no meaning. Transportation networks are shattered. Only fragments of the old world still function—sporadic radio transmissions, tightly controlled military supply chains, and the beginnings of crude barter systems inside survivor enclaves.

Those who live do so by hiding or fortifying. A handful remain in government bunkers. Others occupy abandoned militar
creator: Azazel102
